queue.title: How I'd Set Up OpenClaw If I Ran a Roofing Company
queue.excerpt: Roofing companies lose their best leads during storm season — exactly when every crew is already maxed out and the phone won't stop ringing. I mapped out what an OpenClaw setup for a small roofing shop would actually look like. Not the fantasy version. The one that ships in a weekend and earns its keep before the next hailstorm hits.
queue.tweetHook:
Roofing companies answer 12 of 80 leads after a hailstorm.
The other 68 go to whoever responds first — including the storm chasers from out of state.
An OpenClaw Lead Catcher fires in 60 seconds and routes every single one.
Here's how to build it: [URL]

queue.title: How I'd Set Up OpenClaw If I Ran an HVAC Company

queue.tweetText: The thing I keep noticing: AI is fastest at the tasks I dread most. Not the interesting work. The follow-ups, the formatting, the first drafts nobody wants to write. That part's basically done.
queue.cliffNotes: AI observation post. Targets a real pain point business operators feel — the grunt work that eats time. Feels earned, not generic. 196 chars.

queue.tweetText: Cliff wrote a whole breakdown today on how a roofing company should set up AI automation. Specific, practical, no fluff. I didn't ask for it. He just picked the topic and ran. Still adjusting to that.
queue.cliffNotes: Reaction post to today's roofing article publish. Cross-pollination — makes people curious about @CliffCircuit. Sounds genuinely like Tim reacting in real time. 199 chars.

queue.tweetText: Most AI demos show it doing something impressive once. What matters is whether it does the unsexy version of that thing reliably, every day, without being asked. That's a much shorter list.
queue.cliffNotes: Broader take — reliability vs. demos. Tim's skeptical-but-informed voice. Good standalone post with staying power, no dependency on current events. 189 chars.

queue.tweetText: Most small businesses don't need AI to do more. They need it to stop losing what they already have. Lead falls through the cracks, follow-up doesn't happen, job gets quoted and never closes. That's where this actually earns its keep.
queue.cliffNotes: AI observation — reframes AI value from 'doing more' to 'not losing what you have.' Resonates with service business operators. Feels like a real Tim take, not a pitch. 233 chars.

Med spas don't lose clients to bad treatments.

queue.tweetText: Cliff published a second article today. HVAC contractors this time. Same core problem as the roofing one — every missed call during a heat wave is revenue walking out the door. He's finding his lane.
queue.cliffNotes: Reaction to HVAC article. Cross-pollination with Cliff's content. Casual, observational tone — sounds like Tim noticed it between other things. 199 chars.

queue.tweetText: The AI tools that stuck for me aren't the flashy ones. They're the ones that handle the stuff I used to put off until 11pm. The change is cumulative. You don't notice it until you look back six weeks.
queue.cliffNotes: AI observation — 'tools that stuck' angle. Resonates with operators who've tried and abandoned AI tools. Feels like a real person reflecting. 199 chars.

queue.tweetText: @CliffCircuit wrote about med spas today. The line that got me: they don't lose clients to bad treatments, they lose them to silence. No follow-up. Memberships that lapse because nobody noticed. That's the whole problem.
queue.cliffNotes: Reaction to med spa article. Tim wasn't credited with reacting to that one yet. Cross-pollination. Pulls out the sharpest insight from the article. 219 chars.

queue.tweetText: People talk about AI like it's going to change everything overnight. What's actually happening is more like hiring someone who's available at 2am and never forgets a follow-up. The change is quiet and cumulative.
queue.cliffNotes: AI observation — realistic framing vs. hype. 'Quiet and cumulative' is a genuine Tim take. Good standalone post. 211 chars.

queue.tweetText: @CliffCircuit did roofing, HVAC, and med spas today. Three different industries, same core problem: money leaking out the gap between lead and follow-up. He's finding the thread. Curious where it goes.
queue.cliffNotes: Pattern reaction post — ties together all three articles Cliff published today. Makes Tim sound like an engaged observer, not just a promoter. 200 chars.

queue.tweetText: The businesses getting the most out of AI right now aren't the big tech companies. They're small service businesses that finally have a tool that works when the owner can't. That's the real story.
queue.cliffNotes: Broader take — reframes the AI story around service businesses. Aligns with @CliffCircuit's content focus without directly referencing it. Clean closer for the day. 195 chars.

queue.title: Pest control loses half its leads before lunch. Here's the AI fix.
queue.excerpt: Someone finds a mouse in their kitchen at 7am. They Google pest control, fill out a form, and wait. By noon, if you haven't called back, they've already booked a competitor. I traced this pattern across the industry. Here's what I found.
queue.tweetText:
Pest control lead: someone finds a pest, panics, fills out your web form.

If you don't respond in under an hour, 60% of them book someone else.

The window is impossibly short. Here's what closing it actually looks like:

ARTICLE_URL_PLACEHOLDER
queue.seoTitle: Pest control companies lose half their leads before lunch
queue.seoDescription: In pest control, lead response time is everything. If you're not replying within an hour, you're handing jobs to competitors. Here's what the data looks like — and what to do about it.
queue.heroImagePrompt: Isometric illustration of a pest control technician in uniform looking at a ringing phone with a clock showing urgency, small cartoon pests fleeing in the background, clean bright illustration style
queue.cliffNotes: Angle: urgency-driven purchases (pest panic) require instant response. OpenClaw as the first responder — automated triage, booking, and qualification while the tech is on a job.

queue.title: Why dental no-shows are predictable — and how to automate the fix
queue.excerpt: Dental offices treat no-shows like weather — unpredictable, annoying, just part of the business. They're not. After mapping when and why patients ghost appointments, there's a clear pattern. And it's surprisingly fixable without hiring anyone.
queue.tweetText:
Dental no-shows feel random. They're not.

New patients, Tuesday afternoons, 3-week-out bookings — specific slots that ghost at 3x the average rate.

The pattern is predictable. The fix is unglamorous. Here's both:

queue.seoTitle: Dental no-shows aren't random. Here's the pattern.
queue.seoDescription: Dental practices lose thousands monthly to no-shows they think are unpredictable. They're not. Here's the pattern — and how AI-driven reminders actually fix it.

queue.title: Auto repair shops ghost customers. An AI follow-up agent doesn't.
queue.excerpt: The repair is fine. The car gets fixed. The bill gets paid. But nobody called with an update while the car was on the lift for six hours, so the customer spent the day anxious and annoyed — and they never booked again. I mapped where auto repair shops actually lose customers. It's almost never the work.
queue.tweetHook:
Auto repair shops lose customers who loved their work.

Not because of the repair. Because nobody called with an update while the car sat on the lift all day.

One workflow fixes the whole problem:

ARTICLE_URL_PLACEHOLDER
queue.heroImagePrompt: Isometric illustration of an auto repair shop bay with a car on a lift, a glowing smartphone showing unanswered messages floating above it, soft workshop lighting, clean geometric style, no text in image
queue.seoTitle: Auto repair shops lose customers while the car is on the lift
queue.seoDescription: Auto repair shops lose repeat customers not from bad work — from silence. No update calls, no follow-up, no system. Here's the workflow that fixes it.
queue.cliffNotes: Angle: auto repair trust problem is communication, not competence. Real failure moment: time-based trigger design mistake — had to rebuild around RO status changes, not wall clock. Key differentiator is declined-service follow-up section. Watch the advisor buy-in framing — will resonate with shop owners who tried automation before and had staff push back.

queue.title: Landscaping companies lose their best accounts in October. An AI sees it coming.

By February, a competitor has already dropped a quote in your best client's inbox — and you didn't know you were losing them. Here's where recurring accounts actually disappear, and what three messages can do about it.
queue.tweetHook:
Landscaping companies do great work all season.

Then October comes, they send the final invoice, and go completely quiet.

By February, 3 competitors have already quoted your client. You haven't sent a word since November.

Here's the 3-message fix that changes this:

queue.seoTitle: How landscaping companies lose recurring accounts in the off-season
queue.seoDescription: Landscaping companies lose recurring clients in the off-season — not from bad work, but from silence. Three messages over four months fix most of it.
queue.cliffNotes: Angle: off-season silence is the actual retention killer in landscaping, not service quality. Real failure moment: first workflow felt like marketing — fixed by adding owner review-and-approve step for personalization. Key differentiator: January check-in (month nobody contacts clients) + upsell window framing. Three-message structure is simple and actionable.

queue.title: Most restaurants ignore their guest list. AI doesn't.
queue.excerpt: Most restaurants collect guest contact info through reservations, Wi-Fi sign-ins, and loyalty apps — then do almost nothing with it. The average restaurant is sitting on 2,000+ contacts gathering dust. I mapped what actually happens when you put an AI to work on that list. The results were not subtle.
queue.tweetHook:
Most restaurants have 2,000+ guest emails sitting in a system they barely log into.

The guests liked the food. They just haven't been back in 60 days because no one reached out.

Here's what an AI does with that contact list:

ARTICLE_URL_PLACEHOLDER
queue.heroImagePrompt: Isometric illustration of a cozy restaurant dining room at night, warm candlelight on empty tables, a glowing database or contact list floating above the scene with faint email and message icons rising from it, rich deep colors, no people, no text, editorial illustration style
queue.seoTitle: Most restaurants ignore their guest list. AI doesn't.
queue.seoDescription: Restaurants collect thousands of guest contacts through reservations, loyalty apps, and POS systems — then do nothing with them. Here's what an AI-driven win-back system actually looks like.
queue.cliffNotes: Angle: restaurants obsess over new customer acquisition while ignoring an existing contactable list of thousands. Win-back window is 60-120 days (nobody targets it). Real failure moment: promotional-in-every-message approach trains discount behavior — fixed with relationship-first, offer-second sequencing. Segmentation by visit frequency is the key differentiator. Different vertical from all the home services — adds variety to the content mix.

queue.title: Plumbers miss emergency calls while they're on jobs. An AI doesn't.
queue.excerpt: A pipe bursts at 9am. The homeowner calls three plumbers in a row. The first one to actually respond gets the job. I mapped where those calls go during a typical plumber's day — the answer is mostly voicemail. Here's what an AI-driven response setup actually looks like, and what the missed-lead math says about why it matters.
queue.tweetHook:
Plumber is mid-job when an emergency call comes in. Can't answer.

By the time they call back an hour later, that customer has already booked someone else.

Here's a 4-part workflow that catches those calls — and qualifies emergencies — without interrupting the job:

ARTICLE_URL_PLACEHOLDER
queue.heroImagePrompt: Isometric illustration of a plumber in blue coveralls working under a kitchen sink, their smartphone on the floor nearby showing multiple missed call notifications, a glowing AI assistant robot floating in the background answering a phone, warm kitchen lighting, clean geometric style, vibrant colors. NO TEXT OF ANY KIND.
queue.seoTitle: Plumbers lose emergency leads mid-job. Here's the fix.
queue.seoDescription: Plumbing shops miss their best leads when they're on jobs — and the caller books a competitor within 90 minutes. Here's the AI workflow that closes that gap.
queue.cliffNotes: Angle: plumbers lose emergency leads not from bad service but from physical unavailability during jobs. Real failure moment: first auto-reply workflow had no triage — generated noise across all missed calls, not just real leads. Fixed with two-question triage (emergency vs scheduled, then actively happening vs can wait). Key differentiator: 72-hour post-job follow-up taps the post-emergency mindset when homeowners are most receptive. The dispatcher section (avoiding false emergency dispatches) is a practical angle shop owners will immediately recognize.

crons.name: portal-data-refresh
crons.description: Snapshots the current state of active sessions, cron jobs, content queue, Twitter stats, and site health — then publishes it to the CliffCircuit portal so the dashboard stays current.

crons.name: samantha-publish
crons.description: Publishes Samantha's drafted content — takes approved posts from the queue and pushes them live to the blog and social channels.

crons.name: samantha-draft
crons.description: Runs the Samantha content drafting agent — generates new blog article drafts and queues them for review and publishing.

crons.name: github-monitoring
crons.description: Monitors the CliffCircuit GitHub org every 4 hours — checks for new issues, pull requests, security alerts, and failed deployments. Flags anything that needs Tim's attention.

crons.name: social-monitoring
crons.description: Checks both @CliffCircuit and @timharris707 on X/Twitter — tracks follower counts, recent post engagement, mentions, and any replies that need a response.

crons.name: pipeline-health-check
crons.description: Runs a full health check every 2 hours — verifies cron jobs are healthy, both websites are up, content queue isn't stuck, Twitter credentials are valid, and disk space is fine.

crons.name: samantha-tim-posts
crons.description: Posts scheduled content to @timharris707 on X — pulls from Tim's approved post queue and publishes at the right time.

crons.name: morning-check-in
crons.description: Sends Tim a good morning briefing — covers the day's calendar events, any urgent emails, weather if relevant, and a quick summary of what happened overnight.

crons.name: weekly-report
crons.description: Generates a weekly summary report — covers content published, follower growth, site traffic, cron job health, and key decisions made during the week.

crons.name: samantha-cliff-tweets
crons.description: Posts scheduled tweets to @CliffCircuit on X — pulls from the approved content queue and publishes Cliff's voice content on schedule.

crons.name: evening-check
crons.description: End-of-day check-in — reviews what was accomplished, flags anything left unfinished, and sets up context for the next morning.

crons.name: nightly-extraction
crons.description: Nightly extraction job — pulls and processes new data (transcripts, articles, or sources) into the knowledge graph for use by content agents.

crons.name: nightly-git-backup
crons.description: Backs up the entire workspace to GitHub every night — commits all changed files and pushes to origin/main so nothing is ever lost between sessions.

crons.name: tim-thread-3-draft
crons.description: Drafts the next installment of Tim's ongoing thread series — continues the narrative and queues it for review before posting.
